audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar Balak, Balaam, donkey, and angelic-road narrative tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Moabite fear, curse-for-hire embassy, Balaam's Divine consultation, permission/anger tension, angel as adversary, speaking donkey, animal mistreatment, restricted speech, and Balak's first sacrificial sites.

verses.restored: Children of Israel set out and camped in plains of Moab, across Jordan from Jericho.
Balak son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to Amorite.
Moab was very afraid before the people, for they were many, and Moab felt dread before children of Israel.
Moab said to elders of Midian, Now this assembly will lick up all around us as ox licks up green growth of field. Balak son of Zippor was king of Moab at that time.

He sent messengers to Balaam son of Beor at Pethor, which is by river, to land of sons of his people, to call him, saying: Look, a people came out from Egypt; look, it covers eye of land and it dwells opposite me.

Now, please, come curse this people for me, for it is mightier than I. Perhaps I will be able to strike it and drive it from land, for I know that whom you bless is blessed and whom you curse is cursed.
Elders of Moab and elders of Midian went with payments of divination in their hand; they came to Balaam and spoke to him words of Balak.
He said to them, Lodge here tonight, and I will return word to you as YHWH speaks to me. Leaders of Moab stayed with Balaam.
Divine came to Balaam and said, Who are these men with you?
Balaam said to Divine, Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, sent to me:
Look, the people coming out from Egypt covers eye of land. Now come, curse it for me; perhaps I will be able to fight against it and drive it out.
Divine said to Balaam, You shall not go with them; you shall not curse the people, for it is blessed.
Balaam rose in morning and said to leaders of Balak, Go to your land, for YHWH has refused to let me go with you.
Leaders of Moab rose and came to Balak and said, Balaam refused to come with us.
Balak again sent leaders, more numerous and more honored than these.
They came to Balaam and said to him, Thus says Balak son of Zippor: Please let nothing prevent you from coming to me,
for I will honor you very greatly, and all that you say to me I will do. Come, please, curse this people for me.
Balaam answered and said to servants of Balak, If Balak gives me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot cross mouth/command of YHWH my Divine to do small or great.
Now, please, remain here also tonight, and I will know what more YHWH will speak with me.
Divine came to Balaam at night and said to him, If the men have come to call you, rise, go with them; but only the word that I speak to you, that you shall do.
Balaam rose in morning, saddled his female donkey, and went with leaders of Moab.
Anger of Divine burned because he was going, and messenger of YHWH stationed himself in road as an adversary against him. He was riding upon his donkey, and his two servants were with him.
The donkey saw messenger of YHWH standing in road with drawn sword in his hand; donkey turned aside from road and went into field, and Balaam struck donkey to turn her back to road.
Messenger of YHWH stood in narrow path between vineyards, a wall on this side and wall on that side.
Donkey saw messenger of YHWH and pressed herself against wall, pressing Balaam's foot against wall; he struck her again.
Messenger of YHWH passed farther and stood in narrow place where there was no way to turn right or left.
Donkey saw messenger of YHWH and lay down under Balaam; anger of Balaam burned, and he struck donkey with staff.
YHWH opened mouth of donkey, and she said to Balaam, What have I done to you, that you struck me these three times?
Balaam said to donkey, Because you made a fool of me! If only there were sword in my hand, for now I would kill you.
Donkey said to Balaam, Am I not your donkey, upon whom you have ridden from your beginning until this day? Have I ever been accustomed to do thus to you? And he said, No.
YHWH uncovered eyes of Balaam, and he saw messenger of YHWH standing in road with drawn sword in his hand; he bowed and fell upon his face.
Messenger of YHWH said to him, Why have you struck your donkey these three times? Look, I came out as adversary, for your way is reckless/perverse before me.
Donkey saw me and turned aside before me these three times. If she had not turned aside from me, surely now I would have killed you and kept her alive.
Balaam said to messenger of YHWH, I have sinned/misaligned, for I did not know you were stationed before me in road. Now, if it is evil in your eyes, I will return.
Messenger of YHWH said to Balaam, Go with men, but only word that I speak to you, that you shall speak. So Balaam went with leaders of Balak.
Balak heard that Balaam had come and went out to meet him at city of Moab on border of Arnon, at end of border.
Balak said to Balaam, Did I not surely send to call you? Why did you not come to me? Am I truly not able to honor you?
Balaam said to Balak, Look, I have come to you. Now, am I able to speak anything at all? Word Divine puts in my mouth, that I shall speak.
Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath-huzoth.
Balak sacrificed cattle and flock and sent to Balaam and leaders who were with him.
In morning Balak took Balaam and brought him up to Bamoth-baal, and from there he saw edge of people.

translation_notes: Balak's fear follows Israel's recent victories; it explains the diplomatic crisis but does not justify antisemitic portrayals of Jewish people as an engulfing threat.
Balaam is a foreign diviner who nevertheless receives speech from YHWH; Divine communication is not portrayed as ethnically confined.
The narrative deliberately preserves tension between Divine permission in verse 20 and anger in verse 22; motives, persistence, and the restriction to speak only YHWH's word require literary audit.
The messenger stands as a satan/adversary in the ordinary Hebrew sense, not necessarily the later proper-name figure.
The donkey sees danger before the seer and speaks against repeated violence. The story plainly exposes animal cruelty and Balaam's impaired perception.
Payments of divination and curse-for-hire belong to the narrative; this is not instruction for magical practice.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 22 to promote curses, paid divination, antisemitic fear, animal abuse, or violence toward those who obstruct us. The donkey's perception and protest expose the blindness of coercive power, while Balaam is repeatedly forbidden to speak beyond the word given.
